name: Medications & Triage
sourceAttribution: Compiled from FDA, NIH MedlinePlus, CDC, and public-domain triage guidance (START method). NOT clinical advice — confirm any dose or interaction with a pharmacist or doctor.
license: CC-BY-4.0 (compilation); source citations carry their own public-domain or open license

guides.title: OTC medication categories
guides.sourceRef: FDA Drug Facts label guidance + NIH MedlinePlus Drug information, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
Always follow the Drug Facts label. Don't combine multiple products with the same active ingredient — overdose risk.

Analgesics (pain/fever):
• Acetaminophen (Tylenol) — pain and fever. Gentler on the stomach. Liver toxicity at high doses or with alcohol.
• Ibuprofen (Advil, Motrin) — pain, fever, inflammation. Take with food. Avoid with kidney disease, ulcer history, or 3rd-trimester pregnancy.
• Aspirin — pain, fever, inflammation, blood-thinning. NEVER for anyone under 18 (Reye syndrome risk).

Antihistamines (allergies):
• Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) — sedating; useful at night.
• Loratadine, cetirizine, fexofenadine — non-sedating, daytime allergy use.

Antacids / acid reducers:
• Calcium carbonate (Tums) — immediate relief.
• Famotidine (Pepcid) — H2 blocker, hours of relief.
• Omeprazole, esomeprazole — proton pump inhibitors, daily for chronic reflux.

Antidiarrheals:
• Loperamide (Imodium) — adults only. Do NOT use for bloody diarrhea or high fever (likely bacterial — see a doctor).

For children, dose by weight and use child formulations. Ask a pharmacist if uncertain.

guides.title: Prescriptions you should not stop abruptly
guides.sourceRef: NIH MedlinePlus drug information — discontinuation guidance per drug class, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
Some medications cause dangerous withdrawal or rebound effects if stopped suddenly. Always discuss any change with the prescriber. Examples:

• Beta blockers (heart, blood pressure) — sudden stop can cause rebound tachycardia, hypertensive crisis, even heart attack.
• Antidepressants (SSRIs, SNRIs, etc.) — discontinuation syndrome: dizziness, brain zaps, flu-like symptoms. Taper under guidance.
• Benzodiazepines (Xanax, Ativan, Valium) — withdrawal can cause seizures. Always taper.
• Corticosteroids (prednisone) on long-term use — sudden stop can cause adrenal insufficiency. Taper.
• Thyroid replacement (levothyroxine) — stopping disrupts metabolism over weeks.
• Anticoagulants (warfarin, eliquis, etc.) — sudden stop raises clotting/stroke risk.
• Opioids — withdrawal is dangerous primarily in pregnancy and after long-term use. Taper.
• Anti-seizure medications — stopping can trigger seizures.

Keep at least a 30-day supply on hand. Refill before zero. In a disaster, prioritize prescription continuity — call your pharmacy or doctor's office for emergency refills.

guides.title: Dangerous drug interactions
guides.sourceRef: FDA Drug Interactions guidance + NIH MedlinePlus, retrieved 2026-05-26. Always consult a pharmacist for personal medication review.
guides.content:
Common dangerous combinations:

• Acetaminophen + alcohol — liver toxicity. Limit acetaminophen if drinking, especially heavy/chronic use.
• Warfarin + NSAIDs (ibuprofen, aspirin) — major bleeding risk. Acetaminophen is safer for pain on warfarin.
• MAOIs + many things — other antidepressants, decongestants (pseudoephedrine), tyramine-rich foods (aged cheese). Hypertensive crisis. MAOIs are rare today but still prescribed.
• Grapefruit + statins, calcium channel blockers, some immunosuppressants — alters drug metabolism, raises blood levels.
• Multiple stimulants — ADHD medication + cold medicine + caffeine. Cardiac stress.
• SSRIs + tramadol or other serotonergic drugs — serotonin syndrome.
• Lithium + NSAIDs or diuretics — lithium toxicity.
• Sildenafil (Viagra) + nitrates — severe hypotension.

Keep an updated list of every medication and supplement (including OTC and herbal) with doses. Show it to every provider, every pharmacy. Many interactions only show up when a new drug is added — check before starting anything new.

guides.title: Mass-casualty triage (START method)
guides.sourceRef: START Triage protocol — Newport Beach Fire Department / Hoag Hospital, public domain, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
Used when casualties overwhelm available care. The goal is to sort by who benefits most from immediate care.

Four categories, color-coded:

GREEN — Walking wounded. Move them to a designated holding area. Reassess later.

YELLOW (Delayed) — Alive, not walking, but breathing on their own, has a pulse, and follows commands. Stable for now. Treat after Reds.

RED (Immediate) — Life-threatening but salvageable. Includes: not breathing initially but starts breathing after airway repositioning; respiratory rate over 30/min; capillary refill over 2 seconds OR no radial pulse; cannot follow simple commands.

BLACK (Expectant) — Not breathing even after two airway-clearing attempts. With limited resources, focus moves to others. This decision is brutal; field providers re-evaluate when more help arrives.

Reassess regularly — patients change category as conditions evolve.

guides.title: Wound infection — signs to act on
guides.sourceRef: CDC Wound Infection Prevention + NIH MedlinePlus, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
All wounds are at risk. Clean any wound with clean water (boiled and cooled if you're unsure). Pat dry. Cover with a clean dressing. Change daily or when wet/dirty.

Signs of infection — see a clinician:
• Increasing redness AROUND the wound (some redness at the edge is normal at first; spreading is not)
• Red streaks moving up a limb away from the wound (lymphangitis — can become serious quickly)
• Increasing pain after the first 48 hours (initial pain should improve, not worsen)
• Pus or cloudy discharge
• Warmth, swelling beyond the immediate area
• Fever, chills
• Swollen lymph nodes nearby (groin for leg wounds, armpit for arm wounds)
• Foul smell from the wound

Get medical care urgently for:
• Animal bites (especially cats — high infection rate)
• Puncture wounds (rusty nails, etc.)
• Wounds caused by dirty objects
• Anyone whose tetanus booster is more than 5 years old (or unknown)
• Diabetics — wounds heal slowly and infect easily
• Any wound that's not healing in 1–2 weeks

guides.title: Poisoning — first response
guides.sourceRef: American Association of Poison Control Centers + NIH MedlinePlus, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
In the US, call Poison Control at 1-800-222-1222 (free, 24/7, multilingual). Most poisonings are managed at home with their guidance.

Call your emergency number INSTEAD of poison control if the person is:
• Unconscious or hard to wake
• Not breathing or struggling to breathe
• Seizing
• Severely confused or unresponsive

While waiting:
• Do NOT induce vomiting unless poison control specifically tells you to. Some substances burn going down AND coming back up (corrosives, hydrocarbons).
• Skin/eye contact: rinse with running water for 15–20 minutes.
• Inhalation: get the person to fresh air.
• Keep the container or photo of the substance to show responders.

For opioid overdose, naloxone (Narcan) can reverse it. Spray into one nostril; repeat in the other nostril after 2–3 minutes if no response. Call emergency services even after naloxone — effect wears off and the overdose can return.

For pet poisoning, call ASPCA Animal Poison Control: 888-426-4435 (a fee may apply).
